page: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-24xCPU-256GB is a memory-optimized virtual server running on the KVM hypervisor. It features 24 shared vCPUs mapped to 24 physical cores of an AMD EPYC 7542 processor architecture (x86_64) running at 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 256.0 GB of system memory, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 10.67 GB per vCPU. This instance does not include local storage or bundled GPU resources, and it comes with zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses.

Performance benchmarks indicate a mixed profile. While multi-threaded operations like bzip3 decompression and PassMark CPU physics tests show strong performance in the top 25%, single-core performance is a notable tradeoff, with stress-ng single-core CPU benchmarks scoring in the bottom 10%. Memory performance also presents a bottleneck, as the PassMark memory score falls into the bottom 25%, despite average read and write memory bandwidth benchmarks. General multi-core tasks, software compilation, and database operations consistently perform within the middle 50% average tier.

Given the shared CPU allocation and high memory capacity, this server offers a cost-efficient profile for memory-intensive workloads that do not require sustained, dedicated single-core CPU performance. It is well-suited for large-scale caching layers, multi-threaded data processing, and memory-heavy database operations. However, it is less optimal for single-threaded tasks or workloads requiring high-speed local storage.
description: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-24xCPU-256GB is a memory-optimized virtual server utilizing the KVM hypervisor. It is equipped with 24 shared vCPUs on an AMD EPYC 7542 processor (x86_64) running at 2.0 GHz, paired with 256.0 GB of RAM, yielding 10.67 GB of memory per core. The server does not feature local storage or GPU accelerators. Benchmark data reveals a clear tradeoff: multi-threaded tasks like bzip3 decompression and physics simulations perform strongly in the top 25%, whereas single-core stress-ng performance falls into the bottom 10%, and the PassMark memory score is in the bottom 25%. Most other benchmarks, including database operations and software compilation, rank in the average tier. This hardware profile is qualitatively cost-efficient for memory-heavy, multi-threaded workloads such as caching and database hosting, but is less suitable for single-threaded applications.
